pins.title: Japandi Dining Room with Neutral Decor
pins.description: A Japandi dining room with neutral décor blends Japanese simplicity and Scandinavian warmth. Soft beige, warm wood, and gentle earth tones create a calm, inviting space. Clean lines, natural materials, and minimal furnishings keep the room feeling open and uncluttered, while subtle textures like linen, ceramic, and light wood add quiet elegance and comfort.

vibe_report.summary: Calm, textured Japandi interiors that balance Scandinavian lightness with Japanese restraint. Warm neutrals, light oak, and natural textiles create an uncluttered, family-friendly environment. Prioritize a cozy standing-desk work corner near natural light, low-profile furniture, and subtle plant accents. Preserve all measured architecture and floorplan flow—styling should enhance the existing layout rather than restructure it.
vibe_report.palette_rationale: A warm-neutral palette (#D8B886, #EDE6DB, #CBB79D, #A8A39A, #3B2F2B) reads as sun-warmed oak, soft linen, cream plaster, muted stone, and darker wood accents. Use lighter neutrals on large planes (walls, major textiles) and reserve mid-tones for furniture; the darkest tone is for small sculptural elements and select joinery to anchor composition.
vibe_report.lighting_mood: Soft ambient daylight with directional late-morning sun through bay and patio glazing. Use practical warm pendants and paper lanterns for layered evening mood; keep all light derivation tied to visible windows or fixtures. Shadows should be crisp but gentle—enough shape to show texture on wood and woven surfaces.
materials: wool and natural fiber rugs (jute, hemp blend)
matte plaster or warm off-white paint
blackened metal for small detailing (lamp arms, shelf brackets)
textures: woven rattan / seagrass pendant or basket textures
furniture_language: low-profile sofa with visible wooden base
simple, vertical-back dining chairs in linen
built-in or modular bookshelf with warm finish
minimal standing desk with cable tidiness and small planter
styling_rules: Keep surfaces sparsely curated: 1-3 objects per tabletop (ceramic, book, small plant).
Prioritize vertical wood slat accents and single sculptural wall elements rather than gallery walls.
Use layered neutrals and a single small accent plant per camera frame to add life without clutter.
Ensure textiles read as textured but well-laid: folded throw, arranged cushions, rug edges visible.
All added lighting must have a visible practical source in-frame (pendant, floor lamp, window).
avoid: bright saturated colors or high-gloss finishes
floating furniture placed away from logical circulation paths
introducing new architectural elements or changing window/door placements
lighting that originates from off-frame or impossible directions

room_guidance.headline: Warm, airy family living with a sunny work corner
room_guidance.guidance: Frame the living area as the primary social hub: low linen sofa facing the sculptural coffee table, soft rug underfoot, tall potted plant near the front windows. Add a compact standing desk tucked near the large front glazing or glass door to the Relax patio—orient it so natural light falls across the work surface without screen glare. Add an open bookshelf along an existing wall to define the work corner and hold family objects.

room_guidance.headline: Serene bedroom with tactile simplicity
room_guidance.guidance: Use a simple platform bed with light oak headboard, layered linen bedding in warm cream, and a single low bench at the foot. Place a small reading pendant or wall lamp and a narrow bookshelf for minimal storage. Keep windows unobstructed to show the medium natural light and maintain calm sightlines into the hall.
must_avoid: heavy curtains that block natural light

room_guidance.headline: Quiet functional kitchen that opens to dining
room_guidance.guidance: Highlight natural-material counters and open shelving. Keep the visual connection to the dining bay open; show a few ceramic bowls and a wooden cutting board. Lighting should come from the bay/dining and visible ceiling pendants over the prep zone if present. Avoid staging large appliance clusters on counters—keep surfaces tidy.
must_include: visible pendant or under-cabinet practical light
must_avoid: introducing new kitchen islands or major furniture

room_guidance.headline: Japandi dining bay with woven pendant and round table
room_guidance.guidance: Round oak dining table centered beneath a woven rattan pendant. Use four rounded linen-upholstered chairs. Set a minimal table vignette: single matte ceramic vase and stacked neutral plates. Keep bay windows visible and folded sheers or light blinds that diffuse light but maintain view.
must_avoid: large table centerpiece that blocks sightlines

room_guidance.headline: Calm, spa-like bath with tactile surfaces
room_guidance.guidance: Keep materials matte and natural: pale stone or plaster vanity, wood stool, folded neutral towels. If a small window is present, show soft daylight and a single small plant or wooden tray near the tub. Tone down reflections and glossy tiles.

room_guidance.headline: Bright sunroom/patio for relaxed family moments
room_guidance.guidance: Low seating (bench or lounge) with floor cushions, a small side table, and paper lantern or pendant for evening mood. Show the transition from Nest clearly: doors open to indicate indoor/outdoor flow. Use potted greenery to frame the view outside.
must_avoid: blocking the doors with large items

warnings: Several small service rooms have low natural light—avoid expecting daylight-driven cinematography in those shots.
Measured dimensions available for Dream, Nest, and Dine only; other room sizes are inferred—avoid extreme wide-angle framing that suggests more space than exists.

global_style_notes: Preserve architecture and circulation paths exactly; do not reposition doors/windows in any visualization.

Use low, human-scale camera heights (~42-48 inches) for most interior shots to communicate domestic scale.
Keep styling minimal and functional; every added object must have a logical family use.
